text: The surgeon performs a da Vinci robotic cholecystectomy using three robotic arms. The fundus of the gallbladder is retracted with a striker mini alligator, while two bipolar Maryland instruments are used for the surgical procedure.
The surgeon exposes the cystic particle, with the surrounding peritoneum visibly separated, using robotic instruments.

The surgeon creates a posterior window in the surgical field using the da Vinci robotic system, with the gallbladder fundus retracted by the striker mini alligator and two bipolar Maryland instruments facilitating the procedure.
The surgeon separates the peritoneum up to the body of the gallbladder and proceeds to dissect the cystic duct from the cystic artery using robotic instruments.

The surgeon identifies the anatomical structures within Calot's triangle, with the cystic duct positioned medially and the cystic artery laterally. The common bile duct (CBD) is visible medially, and the inferior edge of the liver is exposed. Proper skeletonization and visualization of Calot's triangle are achieved, and a posterior window is formed.
Two hemoclips are applied to the cystic duct, securing it in preparation for further dissection.
The surgeon applies bipolar cautery to the artery and then uses scissors to cut it, leaving the artery intact before the cut.
The surgeon applies a clip over the cystic artery, ensuring its secure closure. The gallbladder is gradually separated from the liver bed, revealing the underlying tissue planes.
